content: Consider concurrent execution of two transactions $T 1$ and $T 2$ in a DBMS, both of which access a data object $A$. For these two transactions to not conflict on $A$, which one of the following statements must be true?
explanation:
Two operations on the same data object $A$ are non-conflicting only when both are read operations.

Rule for conflict

Two operations conflict if:

  • they belong to different transactions,

  • they access the same data item,

  • and at least one of them is a write operation.

So, for no conflict, neither operation should be a write.

That means:

  • Read-Read: non-conflicting

  • Read-Write: conflicting

  • Write-Read: conflicting

  • Write-Write: conflicting

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: Both $T1$ and $T2$ only read $A$

A schedule of three database transactions $T_1, T_2$, and $T_3$ is shown. $R_i(A)$ and $W_i(A)$ denote read and write of data item $A$ by transaction $T_i, i=1,2,3$. The transaction $T_1$ aborts at the end. Which other transaction(s) will be required to be rolled back?

$$R_1(X) W_1(Y) R_2(X) R_2(Y) R_3(Y) \operatorname{ABORT}\left(T_1\right)$$
explanation: $T_1$ updated data of data item $X$ is reads by $T_2 T_3$ which are uncommitted reads because of $T_1$ failure [ABORT] has to rollback $T_1 T_2$ also. [Cascade Rollbacks]

content: Once the DBMS informs the user that a transaction has been successfully completed, its effect should persist even if the system crashes before all its changes are reflected on disk. This property is called
explanation:
The property being described in your question is known as durability. In the context of database management systems (DBMS), durability is one of the four ACID properties that ensure reliable transaction processing.

Let's briefly explore what each of the ACID properties refers to:

  • Atomicity: This property ensures that a transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit. It either completes in its entirety or not at all. If any part of the transaction fails, the entire transaction is rolled back.
  • Consistency: This property ensures that a transaction brings the database from one valid state to another, maintaining database invariants. Essentially, after a transaction, the database must remain in a consistent state.
  • Isolation: This property ensures that transactions are executed in such a way that they do not interfere with each other. Intermediate states of a transaction are not visible to other transactions.
  • Durability: This property guarantees that once a transaction has been committed, it will persist even in the event of a system crash. The effects of the transaction are permanently recorded in the database.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option A: durability

content: Which of the following statements about the Two Phase Locking (2PL) protocol is/are TRUE?

options.content: With 2PL, a transaction always locks the data item being read or written just before every operation and always releases the lock just after the operation
With 2PL, once a lock is released on any data item inside a transaction, no more locks on any data item can be obtained inside that transaction

explanation:
Let's evaluate each statement one by one to determine whether they are true or false regarding the Two Phase Locking (2PL) protocol.

Option A: 2PL permits only serializable schedules

This statement is TRUE. The Two Phase Locking protocol ensures that the schedules it permits are conflict-serializable. This is achieved by enforcing a protocol where a transaction must acquire all the required locks before releasing any lock.

Option B: With 2PL, a transaction always locks the data item being read or written just before every operation and always releases the lock just after the operation

This statement is FALSE. In the Two Phase Locking protocol, a transaction cannot release a lock until it has acquired all of the locks it needs. Therefore, locks are not released immediately after an operation as implied in this statement.

Option C: With 2PL, once a lock is released on any data item inside a transaction, no more locks on any data item can be obtained inside that transaction

This statement is TRUE. The essence of the Two Phase Locking protocol is that it operates in two distinct phases: the growing phase (where locks can be acquired) and the shrinking phase (where locks are released). Once a lock is released in the shrinking phase, no additional locks can be acquired in that transaction.

Option D: A deadlock is possible with 2PL

This statement is TRUE. While the Two Phase Locking protocol ensures serializability, it does not prevent deadlocks. Deadlocks can occur when two or more transactions hold locks on resources that the other transactions need, leading to a cycle of dependencies that can halt progress.

In summary, the TRUE statements about the Two Phase Locking (2PL) protocol are:

Option A: 2PL permits only serializable schedules

Option C: With 2PL, once a lock is released on any data item inside a transaction, no more locks on any data item can be obtained inside that transaction

Option D: A deadlock is possible with 2PL

content:
Suppose a database system crashes again while recovering from a previous crash. Assume checkpointing is not done by the database either during the transactions or during recovery.

Which of the following statements is/are correct?

options.content: The same undo and redo list will be used while recovering again.
All the transactions that are already undone and redone will not be recovered again.
The system cannot recover any further.

explanation:
In order to recover the database even on multiple crashes the same undo and redo lift will used.

Hence, option (a) is the correct answer.

content:
Consider the following two statements about database transaction schedules:

I. Strict two-phase locking protocol generates conflict serializable schedules that are also recoverable.

II. Timestamp-ordering concurrency control protocol with Thomas Write Rule can generate view serializable schedules that are not conflict serializable.

Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?
explanation:
Statement I:

"Strict two-phase locking protocol generates conflict serializable schedules that are also recoverable."

Analysis:

Strict Two-Phase Locking Protocol (Strict 2PL):

Definition:

Transactions follow the two-phase locking (2PL) protocol:

Growing Phase: A transaction may obtain locks but not release any.

Shrinking Phase: A transaction may release locks but not obtain any new locks.

Strict 2PL adds an additional constraint:

All exclusive (write) locks held by a transaction are released only after the transaction commits or aborts.

This means no dirty reads or writes can occur, as data modified by uncommitted transactions is not visible to others.

Conflict Serializability:

Definition:

A schedule is conflict serializable if it can be transformed into a serial schedule by swapping non-conflicting operations.

Strict 2PL ensures conflict serializability:

By locking, it prevents conflicting operations from different transactions from interleaving in a way that would violate serializability.

Recoverability:

Definition:

A schedule is recoverable if transactions commit only after all transactions whose changes they have read have committed.

Strict 2PL ensures recoverability:

Since uncommitted writes are not visible to other transactions, there is no possibility of cascading aborts or uncommitted data being read.

Transactions cannot read uncommitted data, so recoverability is guaranteed.

Conclusion:

Statement I is TRUE.

Statement II:

"Timestamp-ordering concurrency control protocol with Thomas Write Rule can generate view serializable schedules that are not conflict serializable."

Analysis:

Timestamp-Ordering Protocol (TO):

Definition:

Each transaction is assigned a unique timestamp.

Operations are ordered based on these timestamps.

Basic TO protocol ensures that the order of conflicting operations is consistent with the timestamp order.

Thomas Write Rule:

Definition:

An enhancement to the basic TO protocol that allows certain obsolete writes to be ignored if they do not affect the final outcome.

Specifically, if a transaction $ T_i $ attempts to write to an item that has already been written by a transaction with a later timestamp, $ T_i $'s write can be safely ignored.

View Serializability vs. Conflict Serializability:

Conflict Serializability:

A stricter form of serializability that requires conflicts to be ordered the same way as in some serial schedule.

View Serializability:

A broader notion where the final view (result) is the same as that of some serial schedule, even if conflicts are not ordered identically.

All conflict-serializable schedules are view-serializable, but the converse is not necessarily true.

TO with Thomas Write Rule:

Impact:

By ignoring obsolete writes, the schedule may include operations that, under basic TO, would be considered conflicting but are now permitted.

This can result in schedules that are view serializable but not conflict serializable.

Example:

Suppose we have transactions $ T_1 $ and $ T_2 $ with timestamps TS($ T_1 $) < TS($ T_2 $).

If $ T_1 $ writes to item X, and later $ T_2 $ writes to X, but $ T_1 $'s write is ignored due to Thomas Write Rule, the resulting schedule may not be conflict serializable but remains view serializable.

Conclusion:

Statement II is TRUE.

Final Answer:

Both Statement I and Statement II are TRUE.

Answer: Option A

If $$S$$ is serializable, which one of the following orderings of the vertices of the precedence graph is guaranteed to yield a serial schedule?

read (x) ; x: = x - 50; write (x); read (y); y: = y + 50;   write (y)

The constraint that the sum of the accounts x and y should remain constant is that of

Which of the following concurrency control protocols ensure both conflict serializability and freedom from deadlock?

I. 2-phase locking
II. Time-stamp ordering
Amongst the ACID properties of a transaction, the 'Durability' property requires that the changes made to the database by a successful transaction persist

options.content: Except in case of an Operating System crash
Except in case of a Disk crash
Except in case of a power failure
Always, even if there is a failure of any kind

explanation: Irrespective of failures, the change made by a committed transaction must be permanent.

content: Which level of locking provides the highest degree of concurrency in a relational database?

options.content: Page, table and row level locking allow the same degree of concurrency.

explanation: Row has lower level of granularity( granularity of locks in a database refers to how much of the data is locked at one time) among page, table and row. As in case of Row the amount of data which is locked very less so it takes less amount of memory then many processes can run at the same time. So concurrency is high for Row level locking.

content: Which of the following scenarios may lead to an irrecoverable error in a database system?

options.content: A transaction writes a data item after it is read by an uncommitted transaction
A transaction reads a data item after it is read by an uncommitted transaction
A transaction reads a data item after it is written by a committed transaction
A transaction reads a data item after it is written by an uncommitted transaction

explanation: Reading uncommitted data is called as dirty read. If a transaction reads uncommitted data and commits before the transaction that writes data item leads to non-recoverable schedule if transaction that writes data item rolled back.

content: Consider the database transactions T 1 and T 2, and data items X and Y . Which of the schedule(s) is/are conflict serializable?
explanation: Precedence graphs of each schedule:

content:
Consider the following read-write schedule $S$ over three transactions $T_{1}$, $T_{2}$, and $T_{3}$, where the subscripts in the schedule indicate transaction IDs:

$S: r_{1}(z); w_{1}(z); r_{2}(x); r_{3}(y); w_{3}(y); r_{2}(y); w_{2}(x); w_{2}(y);$

Which of the following transaction schedules is/are conflict equivalent to $S$?
explanation:
Precedence Graph :

Serial Schedules Conflict Equivalent to $ S $ :

$ \begin{array}{lll} T_{1} & T_{3} & T_{2} \quad (T_{1} \rightarrow T_{3} \rightarrow T_{2}) \\ T_{3} & T_{2} & T_{1} \quad (T_{3} \rightarrow T_{2} \rightarrow T_{1}) \\ T_{3} & T_{1} & T_{2} \quad (T_{3} \rightarrow T_{1} \rightarrow T_{2}) \end{array} $

Therefore, the schedules that are conflict equivalent to $ S $ are options B, C, and D.

S : R4(x), R2(x), R3(x), R1(y), W1(y), W2(x), W3(y), R4(y)

Which one of the following serial schedules is conflict equivalent to S?
explanation:
Precedence graph of schedule S.

Topological order = Conflict equal serial order

$$\Rightarrow$$ T1 T3 T4 T2

content:
Let S be the following schedule of operations of three transactions T1, T2 and T3 in a relational database system:

R2(Y), R1(X), R3(Z), R1(Y), W1(X), R2(Z), W2(Y), R3(X), W3(Z)

Consider the statements P and Q below:

P: S is conflict-serializable.

Q: If T3 commits before T1 finishes, then S is recoverable.

Which one of the following choices is correct?

options.content: P is true and Q is false.
Both P and Q are true.
P is false and Q is true.
Both P and Q are false.

explanation:
To determine whether statements P and Q are true or false, let's analyze each one step by step.

Given Schedule $ S $:

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \boldsymbol{T 1} & \boldsymbol{T 2} & \mathbf{T 3} \\ \hline & R_2(y) & \\ R_1(x) & & \\ & & R_3(z) \\ R_1(x) & R_2(z) & \\ W_1(x) & W_2(y) & \\ & & R_3(X) \\ & & W_3(z) \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Statement P:

$ S $ is conflict-serializable.

Conflict-Serializable Analysis:

Identify Conflicting Actions:

Conflicts occur when:

Two operations are on the same data item.

At least one operation is a write.

Operations are from different transactions.

List Conflicting Pairs:

Data Item $ X $:

$ R_1(X) $ and $ W_1(X) $: Same transaction ($ T_1 $); no conflict.

$ W_1(X) $ and $ R_3(X) $: $ T_1 $ writes before $ T_3 $ reads; edge from $ T_1 $ to $ T_3 $.

Data Item $ Y $:

$ R_1(Y) $ and $ W_2(Y) $: $ T_1 $ reads before $ T_2 $ writes; edge from $ T_1 $ to $ T_2 $.

Data Item $ Z $:

$ R_2(Z) $ and $ W_3(Z) $: $ T_2 $ reads before $ T_3 $ writes; edge from $ T_2 $ to $ T_3 $.

Construct Precedence Graph:

Nodes: $ T_1, T_2, T_3 $

Edges:

$ T_1 \rightarrow T_2 $

$ T_1 \rightarrow T_3 $

$ T_2 \rightarrow T_3 $

Check for Cycles:

No cycles are present in the graph.

Conclusion: Schedule $ S $ is conflict-serializable.

Statement Q:

If $ T_3 $ commits before $ T_1 $ finishes, then $ S $ is recoverable.

Recoverability Analysis:

Definition of Recoverability:

A schedule is recoverable if a transaction commits only after all transactions from which it has read have committed.

Identify Reads From Uncommitted Writes:

$ W_1(X) $ by $ T_1 $ at step 5.

$ R_3(X) $ by $ T_3 $ at step 8.

$ T_3 $ reads $ X $ written by $ T_1 $ before $ T_1 $ commits.

Order of Commits:

Given $ T_3 $ commits before $ T_1 $ finishes (and thus before $ T_1 $ commits).

$ T_3 $ has read uncommitted data from $ T_1 $ and commits before $ T_1 $ commits.

Conclusion:

This violates the recoverability condition.

Therefore, $ S $ is not recoverable under the given condition.

Final Answer:

Statement P is true: Schedule $ S $ is conflict-serializable.

Statement Q is false: Schedule $ S $ is not recoverable if $ T_3 $ commits before $ T_1 $ finishes.

Answer: Option A

content:
Let ri(z) and wi(z) denote read and write operations respectively on a data item z by a transaction Ti. Consider the following two schedules.

S1 : r1(x) r1(y) r2(x) r2(y) w2(y) w1(x)

S2 : r1(x) r2(x) r2(y) w2(y) r1(y) w1(x)

Which one of the following options is correct?

options.content: S<sub>1</sub> is conflict serializable, and S<sub>2</sub> is not conflict serializable.
S<sub>1</sub> is not conflict serializable, and S<sub>2</sub> is conflict serializable.
Both S<sub>1</sub> and S<sub>2</sub> are conflict serializable.
Neither S<sub>1</sub> nor S<sub>2</sub> is conflict serializable.

explanation:
To determine whether the given schedules $ S_1 $ and $ S_2 $ are conflict serializable, we analyze each schedule's precedence graph to check for cycles.

Schedule $ S_1 $:

Sequence: $ r_1(x) \, r_1(y) \, r_2(x) \, r_2(y) \, w_2(y) \, w_1(x) $

Precedence Graph for $ S_1 $:

This graph contains a cycle, indicating that $ S_1 $ is not conflict serializable.

Schedule $ S_2 $:

Sequence: $ r_1(x) \, r_2(x) \, r_2(y) \, w_2(y) \, r_1(y) \, w_1(x) $

Precedence Graph for $ S_2 $:

This graph does not contain a cycle, indicating that $ S_2 $ is conflict serializable.

Thus, $ S_1 $ is not conflict serializable due to the cycle in its precedence graph, while $ S_2 $ is conflict serializable, as there is no cycle present.

Option (B) and (D) are false because graphs is not equivalent to given graph.

Option (C) is also false because there is a swapping operation of conflicting operations W1(D)and R2(D)

Hence, the correct option is (A).
